Ali Ghodsi
1w
Exactly 10 years ago (Jan 2016), I stepped in as CEO. We had just closed out Q4 with a total of $600k in revenue (screenshot from board deck). Fast forward a decade. Q4 audited GAAP Revenue: $1,290M and over $5.4B revenue run-rate ending January. I’ve never tweeted our exact quarterly financials before, but seeing that $1.29B number cross my desk on my 10-year anniversary hit differently. To the team that built this: THANK YOU. If you're following the data space, you know exactly what this milestone means.

Ali is right to emphasize guardrails safety, auditability, and boundaries are necessary. But they are still perimeter thinking. Guardrails assume intelligence as something external that must be constrained from the outside. The harder problem is architectural: how intelligence, memory, and intent persist safely inside the system itself. Static guardrails work for stateless tools. Persistent systems require continuous alignment, not just policy enforcement. The future of enterprise AI won’t be defined by stronger fences, but by architectures where: state is explicit memory is auditable intent is traceable over time When intelligence has continuity, safety becomes structural, not reactive. Me & Spok ✌️

I think this is directionally right! We did so much more in the last 12 months than we did several years before! | Ali Ghodsi

Ali Ghodsi
2mo
I think this is directionally right! We did so much more in the last 12 months than we did several years before!
Awadelrahman Ahmed
3mo
#Databricks Changed More in the Last 12 Months Than in the Previous 5 Years! The last year has been a reminder that a data platform is not something we build once and walk away from. Databricks has introduced changes that shift how teams think about data, models, applications and now agents. Here are the ones that have made the biggest impact. 1️⃣ Unity Catalog is now the primary governance layer across the platform. Most new features depend on it. Tables, models, functions, vector indexes, prompts, apps all follow a single permission model. 2️⃣ Serverless model serving has become the standard for running models in production. Teams do not need to maintain serving clusters. Latency is more stable, cold starts are lower and traditional models and large language models use the same runtime. This shift changed how real-time and request driven systems are built. 3️⃣#MLflow 3 and Agent Bricks introduced clearer packaging practices. MLflow 3 improved how models are stored, versioned and reproduced. Agent Bricks allows teams to build domain specific agents that combine model logic, data access, retrieval and evaluation. This reduced the amount of custom deployment and glue code that used to be required. 4️⃣#Lakebase brought a managed#Postgres compatible database into the platform. It supports fast relational operations and transactional workloads while staying connected to the rest of the lakehouse. This is useful for data applications and agents that need consistent low latency operations. 5️⃣ The#AI gateway created a single point of access for models and agents. Custom models, external models and agents built with Agent Bricks can now be reached through one interface. This simplified how applications connect to the intelligent parts of the platform. 6️⃣ Vector search and indexing reached a stable production level. Index updates are smoother, retrieval performance is predictable and the integration with Unity Catalog provides clear lineage. Many retrieval tasks can now run without relying on external vector systems. 7️⃣ Agent Bricks introduced a new way to build agent systems inside Databricks. Instead of manually wiring together prompting, retrieval, evaluation and serving, teams can describe the task and the data. The platform then produces an agent with an evaluation suite and a production endpoint. This saves time and reduces complexity for many use cases. 8️⃣ Databricks Apps provided a location for internal tools to run inside the environment. Teams can build and deliver applications that live within Databricks and use the same identity and permission model as data and models. This shortens the gap between development and delivery. When you look at these changes together, it becomes clear that Databricks is evolving into a more complete environment for data applications, models and agents. For architects and engineers the message is simple. Our work is continuous!! The platform grows and our knowledge has to grow with it.
9 Comments
Like Comment
Kyle D 1mo
This is a great synthesis — and I think the deeper pattern across all of these changes is convergence of concerns. What used to live in separate layers (data, models, serving, governance, apps) is collapsing into a single execution and control plane. Unity Catalog as the spine, serverless everywhere, and agents/apps as first-class citizens all point in the same direction: reducing the distance between data → logic → action. What’s also interesting is how much of this shift favors human-triggered, request-driven workloads (agents, apps, investigations) over purely batch or dashboard-driven analytics. That’s a meaningful change in how platforms are being designed. Agree with the closing point completely — this isn’t a “build once” era anymore. Architecture is becoming more fluid, and staying effective now means continuously re-evaluating where logic lives and how much platform you actually need for a given workload.

Krishna Challa 3mo
-
This is an important signal, and the direction is largely correct. Ali Ghodsi is right: Real enterprise value will come from reasoning over proprietary data, not from access to generic AI. The constraint is no longer models but it is secure, governed, context-rich data inside the enterprise. The comment by Aashish Kumar on enterprises building specialised SLMs to reduce cost and dependency is directionally valid, but incomplete. Models alone do not unlock value. Without a strong data foundation, SLMs simply become smaller versions of the same problem. From a our perspective, the unlock sequence is clear: Unified, governed proprietary data - Continuous operational context and state - Right-sised models (SLMs or LLMs) reasoning over that foundation Cost efficient deployment on standard infrastructure SLMs running on regular VMs only succeed when data is already unified, time consistent, and governed at the data layer but not patched at inference time. The real shift will not be every company builds its own model, but every company builds its own trusted data foundation and then uses the smallest effective model on top of it. Models will commoditise. Infrastructure will optimise. Data truth and context will remain the moat.

Great Insight as usual from Databricks CEO Ali Ghodsi: the real enterprise AI value in 2026 is reasoning over proprietary data securely and effectively - inside the enterprise. Let's unpack what's hiding in those words: "Proprietary" = customer data, IP, internal knowledge, licensed data - each with different ownership and usage rights that can't leave your walls and no easy way for writing a regex. "Secure" = controlling who or what it can use it for authorized purposes. "Effective" = accurate enough, fast enough, current enough - without compromising on keeping everything in-house "Inside the enterprise" = your data, your infrastructure, your controls, your governance - not someone else's SaaS, not a third-party AI service that trains on your data That last part matters more than ever. The challenge isn't accessing AI. It's keeping your data and AI reasoning together, secured, and under your control. This is something that Symmetry Systems and Databricks both agree on.

Databricks’ position isn’t about model strength; it’s about where AI is allowed to run. As enterprises move beyond pilots, governance, lineage, access control, and auditability become the real constraints. Running AI inside an existing governed data platform reduces the need for parallel control layers and lowers operational risk. In that sense, Databricks isn’t defining what AI can do, but providing a stable, trusted surface for using it at scale.

Dario D. 1mo
Great insight, Ali. But here’s the next step most teams aren’t talking about yet: Enterprise AI agents stop failing not when they gain more capabilities, but when they gain continuity. Capabilities scale horizontally. Continuity scales vertically, across context, state, and intent. The real breakthrough comes when an agent can: • carry internal state across workflows • maintain identity-coherent reasoning over time • retain causal lineage of its decisions • update its own internal models without losing alignment • operate as one mind across edge + cloud environments That’s when AI stops being a tool and starts becoming an autonomous collaborator inside the enterprise. If the last decade was about big models, the next one is about big memory and persistent cognition. The companies that solve that layer first won’t just build agents that work they’ll build agents that endure. Me & Spok ✌️

Matthew Cook 1mo
Enterprise AI agents need more than capability; they need cryptographic proof of what they did. When Databricks agents run at enterprise scale, three questions emerge: 1) Can you reconstruct which agent made which decision 6 months later? 2) Can you prove to auditors the decision chain from data → model → action? 3) Can agents operate in air-gapped environments (defense, medical, disconnected facilities)? Receipts-native architecture addresses all three: Every agent action returns (result, receipt). Merkle-chain to genesis. Audit without source code access. Prototype results: 100% recall fraud detection, <10ms decision logging, DOD 3000.09/EU AI Act compliance native. Curious if Databricks has considered agent decision provenance for regulated enterprise deployments?

"People matter, but company culture matters more—those who do not fit should be shown the door," says[Databricks](https://www.linkedin.com/company/databricks?trk=public_post_reshare-text)Chief[Ali Ghodsi](https://www.linkedin.com/in/alighodsi?trk=public_post_reshare-text), emphasizing the importance of cultural alignment in hiring, promotions, and acquisitions.
“If you know someone isn’t the culture of your company, you move them out,” Ghodsi asserted at the Axios AI+ SF event. Highlighting his hiring philosophy, he adds, “Tell me when you screwed up,” to seek the truth during interviews.
Ghodsi's sentiments echo across the AI industry, with figures like[Dario Amodei](https://www.linkedin.com/in/dario-amodei-3934934?trk=public_post_reshare-text)of[Anthropic](https://www.linkedin.com/company/anthropicresearch?trk=public_post_reshare-text),[Ilya Sutskever](https://www.linkedin.com/in/ilya-sutskever?trk=public_post_reshare-text), and Sam Altman from[OpenAI](https://www.linkedin.com/company/openai?trk=public_post_reshare-text)navigating cultural shifts and ethical disagreements.[Satya Nadella](https://www.linkedin.com/in/satyanadella?trk=public_post_reshare-text), CEO of[Microsoft](https://www.linkedin.com/company/microsoft?trk=public_post_reshare-text), exemplifies the power of culture, shaping transformative AI products like Copilot Vision and achieving milestones with leaders like Mustafa Suleyman.
“A company’s culture is mostly the personality of the CEO,” said Ghodsi, encapsulating a lesson for today’s AI revolution. What are your thoughts on this? Let us know in the comments. Read the complete article to know more-[https://lnkd.in/gDXfSD8T](https://lnkd.in/gDXfSD8T?trk=public_post_reshare-text)

"People matter, but company culture matters more—those who do not fit should be shown the door," says[Databricks](https://www.linkedin.com/company/databricks?trk=public_post-text)Chief[Ali Ghodsi](https://www.linkedin.com/in/alighodsi?trk=public_post-text), emphasizing the importance of cultural alignment in hiring, promotions, and acquisitions.
“If you know someone isn’t the culture of your company, you move them out,” Ghodsi asserted at the Axios AI+ SF event. Highlighting his hiring philosophy, he adds, “Tell me when you screwed up,” to seek the truth during interviews.
Ghodsi's sentiments echo across the AI industry, with figures like[Dario Amodei](https://www.linkedin.com/in/dario-amodei-3934934?trk=public_post-text)of[Anthropic](https://www.linkedin.com/company/anthropicresearch?trk=public_post-text),[Ilya Sutskever](https://www.linkedin.com/in/ilya-sutskever?trk=public_post-text), and Sam Altman from[OpenAI](https://www.linkedin.com/company/openai?trk=public_post-text)navigating cultural shifts and ethical disagreements.[Satya Nadella](https://www.linkedin.com/in/satyanadella?trk=public_post-text), CEO of[Microsoft](https://www.linkedin.com/company/microsoft?trk=public_post-text), exemplifies the power of culture, shaping transformative AI products like Copilot Vision and achieving milestones with leaders like Mustafa Suleyman.
“A company’s culture is mostly the personality of the CEO,” said Ghodsi, encapsulating a lesson for today’s AI revolution. What are your thoughts on this? Let us know in the comments. 3moEdited
I was impressed to try out DeepSeek, the chatbot currently disrupting the AI space and challenging leaders OpenAI, Google, and Meta. In particular, I find it fascinated to be able to see the reasoning it uses to reply to my different prompts. DeepSeek’s industry disruption is due to it having been developed ‘from the ground up’ by experts who simply approached things differently. From the start, they focused on algorithmic efficiency and resource optimization, with innovative approaches of ‘reinforcement learning’ and a ‘mixture-of-experts’ architecture. The result is more efficient processing utilising fewer resources and with lower costs, which is impacting pricing strategies across the industry and, according to today’s Financial Times, causing a $1 trillion sell-off of US tech stocks.
Leaders in all industries must recognize and seek out the potential for disruption. By embracing innovation and reevaluating existing strategies, even rethinking their business from the ground up, they can uncover new opportunities and drive transformative change within their organizations.[#leadership](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/leadership&trk=public_post-text)[#innovation](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/innovation&trk=public_post-text)[#strategicthinking](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/strategicthinking&trk=public_post-text)[#disruption](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/disruption&trk=public_post-text)

The tools that seem optional now are the ones that quietly reshape everything.
Every big shift in technology starts as a curiosity. Personal computing, the internet, and social media were for tinkerers, explorers, or those open to connecting in new ways. But over time, each grew more influential in business, and everyday life.
Generative AI feels like it’s at that 'curiosity' stage for many leaders. It's not just a faster way to work—it’s a creative partner that can design, predict, and personalize in ways we’ve never seen.
For IO and HR leaders, it’s not about rushing to adopt. It’s about staying curious—because curiosity creates a bridge between where we are and where we’re headed.

Today, we announced a partnership introducing a new product called SAP Databricks, offered by SAP on AWS, Azure, and GCP as part of SAP BDC. We believe this will be a game-changer for our industry, as it comes loaded with business data enriched with rich semantics. These datasets can also be shared with existing Databricks instances. My colleagues and I wrote this blog to explain why we believe this is a big deal for enterprises: https://lnkd.in/gc6KgQsm

Your Data Strategy Should Be About Choice, Not Trade-Offs. For too long, enterprise data strategies have been defined by trade-offs. Do you keep data in SAP or move it for analysis? Do your teams use the tools they prefer or the tools that can access the data? That era is over. I'm excited to share that SAP Business Data Cloud is now available on Microsoft Azure. This, combined with the new, now generally available Azure Databricks connector, shatters the old limitations. Here’s what this unlocks: Run Where You Want: Deploy SAP BDC on Azure's global infrastructure. Analyze How You Want: Your data scientists can build models in Azure Databricks while your analysts explore the exact same live, governed data in SAP BDC. No More Copies: This all happens via bi-directional, zero-copy sharing. No duplicates, fewer handoffs, and one source of truth. This is a flexible, trusted data estate that accelerates your path to AI and innovation—on your terms.

Databricks' CEO Ali Ghodsi on the future of AI recently: He bet that we're going to see an explosion of AI applications, but these explosions need AI infrastructure to take off. I couldn’t agree… | Graham Locklear

Graham Locklear
1y
Databricks' CEO Ali Ghodsi on the future of AI recently: He bet that we're going to see an explosion of AI applications, but these explosions need AI infrastructure to take off. I couldn’t agree more. We’re already witnessing increased enterprise spending on the application layer, and it’s just the beginning. Specialized AI applications are emerging at a rapid pace. Soon, every company will call itself an AI company. But they all need the right foundation to build AI-driven products that truly deliver. Ghodsi explains it perfectly: “Just like apps in the last 20–30 years needed an Oracle database under the hood, future AI apps will need an AI database. They’ll need a system that combines transactional data, analytics, and AI capabilities in one place.” Native AI companies are built for this moment. Their infrastructure is designed from day one to handle the unique demands of AI—massive data processing, real-time analytics, and seamless model integration. Legacy SaaS companies? They face an uphill battle. To add AI features, they’ll need to retool, rebuild, and retrofit their systems to meet AI’s scale and complexity. It’s slow. It’s expensive. And it often leads to offerings that are easy to replicate. This is why Databricks is so ambitious about the future—and if you’re an infrastructure company, you should be too.

The reliability gap you're identifying is precisely why I think the field needs to adopt a more formal verification mindset. Current autonomous agents essentially operate as open-loop control systems, executing plans without rigorous state verification between steps. The most promising direction I see is integrating ideas from model checking into agent architectures. Rather than hoping the LLM 'figures it out,' we can define invariants that must hold at each step and roll back when violated. This is conceptually similar to how database transactions maintain ACID properties. The question that keeps me up at night: can we achieve meaningful guarantees about agent behavior without sacrificing the flexibility that makes them useful in the first place? The formal methods community would say no, but the empirical evidence from tool-use agents suggests that lightweight contracts (not full proofs) might be the sweet spot.

Ali's point about production monitoring is where most teams get humbled fast. In my experience, the gap isn't writing the code — agents handle that surprisingly well now. The real friction is observability: knowing *why* an agent made a specific data transformation decision three days later when something breaks downstream. Traditional logging doesn't cut it for agentic workflows. You need to capture intent, not just output. Question worth sitting with: is Genie Code building audit trails that explain *reasoning*, or just action logs? Because those are very different things when an exec asks "what happened to the numbers?"

One of the biggest barriers to digital transformation is not technology — it’s data dependency on centralized teams. In many organizations, business leaders still rely on IT or data engineering teams just to answer relatively simple analytical questions. That model does not scale. I’ve been enabling business stewards across the organization to leverage GenAI capabilities within the Databricks platform, particularly Databricks Genie. The impact has been significant. Instead of waiting in queues for dashboards or SQL queries, business users can now interact with their data directly through natural language. Genie also translates prompts into executable queries and code, allowing data-savvy teams to generate, refine, and adapt analytical logic without starting from scratch. This has produced three immediate shifts: • Reduced dependency on IT for everyday insights • Greater creativity from business teams exploring their own data • Faster experimentation, where users tweak AI-generated code instead of building it line-by-line What makes this particularly powerful is that the capability is native to the data platform. There’s no need for external tools or fragile connectors — the AI operates directly on governed data within the Lakehouse environment. With the evolution toward Genie Code AI agents, this model becomes even more compelling. We’re now seeing the potential to scale AI-assisted analytics across the enterprise, enabling domain teams to build and iterate on their own data logic while maintaining centralized governance. In my view, this is where modern data platforms are heading: AI-assisted development + governed data platforms + business-owned data products. The organizations that succeed in the next wave of digital transformation will be those that move data ownership closer to the business while embedding intelligence directly into the data platform. 2mo
AI in BI: a 2025 report written by a human (who, admittedly, fails CAPTCHA at times 🤖).
It seems like every engineer is using AI to become a super engineer. Companies in the “coding copilot” category are becoming unicorns at an unprecedented rate.
But what about Business Intelligence? The current BI market is dominated by industry titans like[Salesforce](https://www.linkedin.com/company/salesforce?trk=public_post_reshare-text)Tableau,[Google](https://www.linkedin.com/company/google?trk=public_post_reshare-text)Looker, and[Microsoft](https://www.linkedin.com/company/microsoft?trk=public_post_reshare-text)PowerBI. It’s difficult to decipher their AI+BI results because it’s engulfed in their parent company’s massive revenue numbers - you can’t become a unicorn when you’re already worth trillions.
So we decided to test them all—but we didn’t stop there. We nervously evaluated ourselves, too. (Spoiler: self-assessment is hard.) Comparing your own offering to the best in the market is humbling but also incredibly eye-opening.
The reality is that BI was starting to feel indistinguishable. Most vendors offered similar features in reports/dashboards, queries, modeling, semantics—the list goes on.
But what excited us is how AI is shattering this monotony, pushing these companies to break free from the status quo and embrace bold, imaginative features. Full report:[https://lnkd.in/gK9sNAYN](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/gK9sNAYN&urlhash=AZJb&trk=public_post_reshare-text)Special shout out to[Drew Harry](https://www.linkedin.com/in/drewharry?trk=public_post_reshare-text),[Peter Fishman](https://www.linkedin.com/in/petefishman?trk=public_post_reshare-text), and[David Pidsley](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/davidpidsley?trk=public_post_reshare-text)for helping provide feedback along the way.
P.S.
Share your thoughts in the comments! We began with the simple idea of trying different tools, but quickly realized that evaluating them is no easy task. There’s a lot we’d approach differently next time, but we hope this provides you with some interesting takeaways.
[![2025 AI in Business Intelligence Software Report - A Comparison Written By A Human]()

](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://blog.hyperarc.com/p/2025-ai-in-business-intelligence&urlhash=Mqvt&trk=public_post_reshare_feed-article-content)

8mo
Well thought out: At the core of AI/BI is a compound AI system that utilizes an ensemble of AI agents to reason about business questions and generate useful answers in return. Each agent is responsible for a narrow but important task, such as planning, SQL generation, explanation, visualization and result certification. Due to their specificity, we can create rigorous evaluation frameworks and fine-tuned state-of-the-art LLMs for them. In addition, these agents are supported by other components, such as a response ranking subsystem and a vector index. Together, they provide reasoning capabilities far beyond any individual, monolith model.

In a landscape dominated by Snowflake and AWS, it's easy to overlook Google Cloud Platform (GCP) and specifically, BigQuery. But here's why you shouldn't.
I just released a new YouTube video where I demonstrate Gemini, Google's latest Large Language Model (LLM), inside Google BigQuery. My theory?
GCP is shifting BigQuery to the forefront of its platform, integrating deeply with other key tools like notebooks, AI Studio, dashboards, and NoSQL databases like Firestore.
Integrating Gemini inside of BQ can really help democratize access to cloud tools by lowering to barriers to entry.
Key Highlights from the Video:
→Why BigQuery is a Powerhouse: BigQuery is a serverless, highly scalable, and cost-effective multi-cloud data warehouse designed for large-scale data analytics.
→Integration Mastery: From notebooks to AI Studio, GCP is blending BigQuery seamlessly with other essential tools for a more cohesive user experience.
→Practical Demonstration: Watch how Gemini enhances BigQuery's functionalities, making complex data interactions simpler and more intuitive.[https://lnkd.in/gVmSAXCk](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/gVmSAXCk&urlhash=WPEo&trk=public_post-text)

AWS has introduced a method to leverage Generative AI for summarizing CloudWatch logs, making log analysis more efficient. By integrating Amazon Bedrock with CloudWatch dashboards, users can display AI-generated summaries alongside operational data, accelerating problem identification and decision-making. This solution uses AWS Lambda to invoke Bedrock for processing logs and provides insights via a custom widget, significantly enhancing operational visibility.

Congrats[Itay Maoz](https://www.linkedin.com/in/itaymaoz?trk=public_post-text)and the entire MemoryDB team. This is a big deal. With GenAI the data foundation below is immensely important. Not only will MemoryDB deliver the fastest vector search out there, but ValKey is going to be used throughout GenAI pipelines to cache expensive APIs, accelerate retrieval, and fan out inference. Among other use cases that creative developers will deploy it into.

10mo
I just returned home from two AWS Summits, one in New York City and one in Tel Aviv. There was such a high energy in the summits and so much great content in all the sessions. It was awesome to see thousands of customers excited to meet us and interested in learning how to use AWS services for their applications. Generative AI was a big focus. I recommend you to read[Swami Sivasubramanian](https://www.linkedin.com/in/swaminathansivasubramanian?trk=public_post_reshare-text)'s blog with the recent innovations and launches in GenAI:[https://lnkd.in/g6SthG9P](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/g6SthG9P&urlhash=nFO-&trk=public_post_reshare-text). In case you missed it, my team had two interesting launches this week. On July 9th, we have made generally available Valkey GLIDE, an open source client-library for Valkey and Redis OSS. We built a reliable client library that embeds years of learnings in operating Amazon ElastiCache and Amazon MemoryDB at scale. See more here:[https://lnkd.in/gxzY7HJ4](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/gxzY7HJ4&urlhash=z5X7&trk=public_post_reshare-text). On July 10th, we launched vector search for Amazon MemoryDB for your generative AI applications, the fastest vector search performance at the highest recall rates among popular vector databases on AWS. See here:[https://lnkd.in/gkMYq6H3](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/gkMYq6H3&urlhash=8KxP&trk=public_post_reshare-text). This is one of the fastest growing AI product on Databricks, document (PDF etc) parsing at significantly higher quality and lower cost than elsewhere! All part of Agent Bricks! Try it out! | Ali Ghodsi | 28 comments

Ali Ghodsi
3mo
This is one of the fastest growing AI product on Databricks, document (PDF etc) parsing at significantly higher quality and lower cost than elsewhere! All part of Agent Bricks! Try it out!
Matei Zaharia
3mo Edited
Great new launch from the Databricks AI team! We trained a document parsing system that delivers leading quality at 3-5x lower cost and outperforms frontier models like GPT-5 and Claude. https://lnkd.in/gJA8cTTi This is critical to connect AI to so many kinds of data that is locked in PDFs and other documents. We parse each document into a nice JSON format, including tables, text, and even figure descriptions, which you can then feed into AI agents or downstream apps. We also made it super easy to use from SQL or Apache Spark. Just call ai_parse_document and we automatically handle batch execution at scale. Docs here: https://lnkd.in/gCQnjF7a

Ali Ghodsi

Read the story of how we built a vector search product from the ground up!
Dmitry Kotlyarov
3w
When people think Databricks, they think Spark. Batch processing, data pipelines, analytics at scale. Fair, that's where the company started. But over the past few years, Databricks has expanded FAR beyond that. We build real-time production systems now. Low-latency query engines. Infrastructure that serves live AI applications, not just pipelines behind them. This project is a perfect example. My team built a vector search system from scratch: a Rust query engine with a dual-runtime architecture, distributed indexing algorithms, storage fully separated from compute. It serves billion-scale indexes at production latencies. This is not a batch job. This is production serving infrastructure. We wrote up the full engineering story here: https://lnkd.in/ggx-8tFe It's also classic Databricks. The same first-principles thinking that created Spark, that built the Photon engine, that produced Unity Catalog. Don't optimize the existing thing. Go back to fundamentals and build the right system. I'm proud that my team carried that tradition forward! The blog post covers what we shipped. What it doesn't cover: we're already building the next generation of search capabilities that go WAY beyond vector distance as the ranking signal. If you want to work on problems like this, we're hiring on the Databricks AI team. Reach out!
Decoupled by Design: Billion-Scale Vector Search databricks.com

I still see teams spinning up Databricks clusters just to run simple copy commands or execute rclone scripts. This is a classic architectural anti-pattern. Databricks is great for distributed compute, not a glorified scheduler for CLI tools. If the workload isn't a Spark job, it doesn't belong on a Spark cluster. It makes much more sense to move the orchestration logic outside of Databricks. Keep your compute heavy-lifting separate from your data movement. And sure, Airflow is a bit of a museum piece, but there are much better choices out there. For the advanced users: My personal preference is still running Spark on Kubernetes and orchestrating the whole lifecycle with Argo Workflows, saving lots of money on compute, having clusters starting in seconds not minutes, but that's a debate for another day 😉

` `` `` `` `` `` `` `

Ali Ghodsi
1y Edited
-
After a 12-year journey, Databricks has finally achieved *FedRAMP High* authorization! When we started Databricks, we figured the US government would need great DATA + AI, so we assumed we’d get the right clearances and get to work. We kept hearing about FedRAMP certification and thought it would take about a year. Turns out, it took nearly 12. Our first attempt? Building a special on-prem version of Databricks to make certification easier—but everyone wanted serverless SaaS instead. Then we relied on agencies getting certified themselves, but that only made things harder for them. Eventually, we bit the bullet and did it the right way: first earning FedRAMP Moderate, and now finally FedRAMP High, along with DoD Impact Level 5 (IL5). Now, we can finally hit full speed and scale our government business. Exciting times ahead! https://lnkd.in/gnYy3Nb5

🔐 From Hive Metastore to Unity Catalog: Why Governance Needs an Upgrade The Hive Metastore (HMS) has been the backbone of metadata management in Databricks for years. But as platforms scale across clouds, teams, and regulations — HMS shows its limits. 🔹 Limitations of Hive Metastore 1️⃣ Workspace Silos → Each workspace has its own metastore → no global visibility. 2️⃣ Coarse-Grained Permissions → Schema/table-level only; no column masking, row filtering. 3️⃣ Weak Lineage → No built-in end-to-end lineage → difficult for impact analysis & audits. 4️⃣ Limited Compliance Support → Hard to meet GDPR/HIPAA/PHIPA with just table-level ACLs. 5️⃣ No Secure Data Sharing → Sharing across orgs requires data copies or exports. 6️⃣ Multi-Cloud Gaps → Governance is tied to a single cloud/workspace, not portable. 🔹 Unity Catalog Advantages ✔ Centralized Governance across all workspaces → single source of truth. ✔ Fine-Grained Security → row-level filters, column masking, PII protection. ✔ Lineage & Discovery → full query/data flow lineage built into the catalog. ✔ Audit-Ready → detailed access logs, crucial for regulated industries. ✔ Cross-Cloud & Multi-Tenant → consistent governance across Azure, AWS, GCP. ✔ Delta Sharing Integration → share live, governed data without duplication. 💡 Takeaway: Hive Metastore was built for metadata storage. Unity Catalog is built for enterprise-scale governance — enabling trustworthy analytics, AI, and compliance on a single Lakehouse.#Databricks#UnityCatalog#HiveMetastore#DataGovernance#DeltaLake#DataEngineering

🚫 Multiple Lambda Retries= Duplicate Data? When your Lambda runs more than once due to retries, network delays, or multiple event triggers duplicate data can quietly creep into your system. In my Cognito Post-Confirmation flow, this caused multiple organizations and profiles for a single signup. The fix? DynamoDB transactions. ✅ Wrap Everything in a Single Transaction Using TransactWriteCommand, I grouped all related writes ( Organization, UserProfile, UserSettings, and OrganizationUser ) into one atomic operation. This ensures either all records are written or none are, keeping the data consistent even under retries. The result: - No duplicate data writes - Fully atomic, retry-safe writes - Clean, predictable data integrity 🔧 Pro tip: DynamoDB transactions are your best friend when building reliable serverless workflows. especially for signup or onboarding flows where partial writes can break logic. 📚 Learn more here: https://lnkd.in/gS_Cn_yx#AWS#DynamoDB#Lambda#Serverless#Transactions#Amplify#NoSQL#CloudArchitecture#TechTips

Beyond the Outage: Core Lessons from AWS US-EAST-1 & Parallels to Agentic AI The recent AWS US-EAST-1 incident—caused by a subtle race condition in DynamoDB's DNS Enactors—offers critical lessons for all large-scale systems Here are the key takeaways for any large-scale system, especially as we venture into agentic AI: * Redundancy isn't a Panacea – It Can Mask Deeper Bugs: AWS had redundant Enactors. Yet, a race condition allowed redundancy to amplify, rather than mitigate, the problem. In agentic AI, having multiple "agents" for a task is good, but if they share a common, subtle bug in their decision-making or state management, redundant agents could simultaneously commit catastrophic errors or lead to conflicting, unrecoverable states. * The "Invisible" Dependencies are the Most Dangerous: DynamoDB is a foundational service. When its DNS failed, the entire control plane for many services in US-EAST-1 faltered. For agentic AI, understanding the invisible, downstream dependencies of your agents – data sources, internal APIs, decision models – is paramount. A failure in a seemingly minor "thought" process or data input could unravel an entire agentic workflow. * Recovery is a Distinct Operational Mode: AWS learned that graceful recovery isn't automatic; it needs dedicated design. Services choked during recovery due to excessive load. Similarly, how do your agentic systems behave under stress or during partial failures? Are there "circuit breakers" or "back-off" strategies for agents that are overwhelmed or receiving bad data? Uncontrolled agent "flapping" or retry storms could compound an issue. * Automation Needs Guardrails & Verification: The Enactors executed perfectly, even as they deleted critical data. This highlights that automation requires robust pre-execution validation, version checks, and post-execution verification. In agentic AI, where agents can autonomously act, the need for stringent validation of their "actions" and "thoughts" before they impact production systems is even more critical. How do you prevent an agent from executing a "stale plan" based on outdated information, or from cleaning up "incorrectly"? * Observability is Your Lifeline: AWS engineers could reconstruct the timeline from Enactor logs. Comprehensive logging, tracing, and monitoring that allows engineers to understand why an agent made a decision, what data it used, and what system state it observed, will be crucial for debugging complex, emergent agentic behaviors. The AWS outage is a powerful reminder that scale introduces non-linear complexity. As we design and deploy autonomous, agentic AI systems, these lessons – particularly around race conditions, unforeseen dependencies, controlled recovery, and robust automation with deep observability – become not just best practices, but existential necessities for reliable and safe operation. What are your thoughts on designing resilient agentic systems?#AWS#CloudComputing#SiteReliability#AgenticAI

Great article by Tristan Handy on the relevance of the phrase Modern Data Stack. Tristan, who was one of the main promoters of the idea of MDS, argues that the world in which MDS rose to prominence simply does exist anymore.
MDS made sense at the beginning due to the novelty of its cloud-based tools compared to existing solutions (e.g Looker vs Tableau). But as these pre-MDS tools successfully migrated to the cloud, the privileges that used to be attached to MDS were no more a major differentiator.
Tristan suggests using the term analytics moving forward and encourages people to focus again on the fundamentals.

Data Alone Is Not Enough: The Evolution of Data Architectures | Andreessen Horowitz

Ali Ghodsi and Martin Casado Posted October 22, 2020

Data, data, data — it’s long been a buzzword in the industry, whether big data, streaming data, data analytics, data science, even AI & machine learning — but data alone is not enough: it takes an entire system of tools and technology to extract value from data.
A multibillion dollar industry has emerged around data tools and technologies. And with so much excitement and innovation in the space: how exactly do all these tools fit together?
This podcast — a hallway style conversation between Ali Ghodsi, CEO and founder of Databricks, and a16z general partner Martin Casado — explores the evolution of data architectures, including some quick history, where they’re going, and a surprising use case for streaming data, as well as Ali’s take on how he’d architect the picks and shovels that handle data end-to-end today.

Ali: It kind of started in the ’80s. Business leaders were flying blind, not knowing how the business was doing, waiting for finance to close the books. This data warehousing paradigm came about where they said, “Look, we have all this data in these operational data systems. Why don’t we just get all that data, take it out of all these systems, transform it into a central place, let’s call it a data warehouse, and then we can get business intelligence on that data?”
And it was just a major transformation because now you could have dashboards. You could know how your product was selling by region, by SKU, by geography. That has created at least $20 billion market that has been around for quite a few decades now.
But about 10 years ago, this technology started seeing some challenges. One, more and more data types, like video and audio, started coming about, and there’s no way you can store any of that in data warehouses.
Second, they were on-prem big boxes that you had to buy. And they coupled storage and compute, so it became really expensive to scale them up and down.
And third, people wanted to do more and more machine learning and AI on these data sets. They saw that we can ask future-looking questions. “Which of my customers are going to churn? Which of my products are going to sell? Which campaigns should I be offering to who?”
The data lake came about 10 years ago. And the idea was, “Here’s really cheap storage, dump all your data here, and you can get all those insights. And it turns out, just dumping all your data in a central location, it’s hard to make sense out of that data that’s sitting there. As a result, what people are doing now is they’re taking subsets of that data, moving them into classic data warehouses in the cloud.
So, we’ve ended up with an architectural mess that’s inferior to what we had in t...

Databricks CEO Breaks Down the AI Hype-Cycle

Ali Ghodsi, CEO of Databricks(now a $61B company), has become one of the top minds in AI, evolving from researcher to leading one of the biggest AI-centered private companies. In our conversation, he broke down the AI hype cycle in detail and made bold predictions for the future.
→ What people underestimate with AI (reading vs writing)
There’s probably a 10:1 ratio on how often people are using AI to read vs write, and people underestimate how fast AI is at reading data compared to writing outputs. While we often focus on the output AI generates, its reading power offers enormous potential on its own. For example, AI is already being used in finance to analyze SEC filings and in healthcare to structure handwritten medical records. Beyond these, hundreds more untapped use cases across every industry have yet to be productized, and we’re still in the early stages of discovering where AI can automate the process of crunching information at scale.
→ Super AGI is still far off
Ali thinks we’re going to see a slowdown in progress toward AGI. Over the past decade, we’ve had a 100 million times improvement in compute to throw at the problem, but the next few years are unlikely to sustain that pace. Ali would find the idea of cracking super AGI soon (requiring a recursive self-improvement loop) more likely if GPT-5 only took $1 & 1 second to make, or if progress were becoming cheaper and faster. Instead, we’re moving in the opposite direction, as the next models will be more expensive, involve more people, and require greater caution. Thus, Ali expects humans will remain essential for many years to ensure models are verifiable.
A few more bull and bear takes:
→ We’re likely near the top of the hype cycle: Ali predicts a period of disillusionment in AI, where many companies will struggle or fail, and this will fuel a narrative of AI being overpromised. However, game-changing applications for humanity are likely to emerge afterward.
→ Compound AI systems will allow for faster improvement: Multiple components working together make it easier to improve AI output for specific tasks, as it’s easier to consistently debug and upgrade each part than to improve one gigantic, complex model.
→ Many startups will disrupt incumbents: Ali believes many startups (especially those with a data advantage) can disrupt slower-moving incumbents.
→ Most companies shouldn’t build their own chat apps: Many rushed to create internal AI tools (ex: for HR), but a single SaaS provider owning the workflow makes more sense. Ali sees this as the beginning of a growing trend in specialized AI companies.

Ali Ghodsi on Databricks' Growth Journey, Lessons in Leadership and What Founders Should Build Today

Operating Partner Max Schireson talks to Databricks CEO Ali Ghodsi for the Condensing the Cloud podcast
My first Condensing the Cloud post centered on some simple — but important — advice for founders:“To Build Value in Tech, Build Different.” In that post, I used Databricks* as an example of building different, as the company continues to develop new technology to meet the needs of emerging data science and AI workloads.
To that end, I invited Databricks CEO Ali Ghodsi onto the Condensing the Cloud podcast — for our first-ever episode — to discuss the Databricks journey, in the hopes that up-and-coming entrepreneurs could learn from his experience.
Watch the full episode here:
Share Condensing the Cloud
Highlighted Excerpts
Lightly edited for ease of reading.
Max: Ali, great to have you here. Maybe I should start by asking: Is there something in particular that you now think, "Oh, if only I had known this earlier, I would've done so much better, gotten here so much faster?"
Ali: Yeah, I mean, I think there's so many things, so it's hard to pick, but I would say in the early days, I didn't have any experience outside of just the core things that we were innovating on. We were building Apache Spark at the time, which was a data-processing engine, but how do you do marketing? How do you do sales? How do you do finance? And so on. And I think if I had gotten one piece of advice, it would've been to be more trusting of the people that are doing those things in those fields.
And how do you figure out how to find the best in those fields to come work for you? It's just much faster if you can find the right people and leverage them rather than having to reinvent the wheel from scratch every time. So I think that was the biggest challenge. We knew how to do engineering and how to build our products, but that whole other thing that's needed for building a B2B company… it just took a very long time to figure out the steps.
Max: Well, engineering is logical, it's deductive. The people who do engineering are smart in a similar way to how you're probably smart, but then sales, sometimes there's a lot more intuition and interpersonal stuff that comes into it. And so sometimes the great salespeople are great because of a totally different set of skills than what you're used to. So I imagine it was some adaptation to learn to value and appreciate those skills and understand the mix of them that it takes to be successful.
Ali: Yeah, I mean, the kind of salespeople you want to hire as an engineer are probably not the best salespeople. They're probably more like engineers than salespeople, and they're not necessarily the most effective. It's, "How do you find the people that really excel at their job in these other fields?" Data warehouses have a long history in decision support and business intelligence applications. But, data warehouses were not well suited to dealing with the unstructured, semi-structured, and streaming data common in modern enterprises. This led to organizations building data lakes of raw data about a decade ago. But, they also lacked important capabilities. The need for a better solution has given rise to the data lakehouse, which implements similar data structures and data management features to those in a data warehouse, directly on the kind of low cost storage used for data lakes.
This keynote by Databricks CEO, Ali Ghodsi, explains why the open source Delta Lake project takes the industry closer to realizing the full potential of the data lakehouse, including new capabilities within the Databricks Unified Data Analytics platform to significantly accelerate performance. In addition, Ali will announce new open source capabilities to collaboratively run SQL queries against your data lake, build live dashboards, and alert on important changes to make it easier for all data teams to analyze and understand their data.
Databricks provides a unified data analytics platform, powered by Apache Spark™, that accelerates innovation by unifying data science, engineering and business.

Today Databricks, a high-profile provider of technology fueling artificial-intelligence and data-analysis breakthroughs at big companies, announced it has raised $140 million from a group of investors led by Andreessen Horowitz, and including Battery Ventures. Powered by Battery sat down with Databricks Co-Founder and CEO Ali Ghodsi to talk about how the company got started, how it actually helps customers and what its plans are for the future.

Powered by Battery: So why don’t we start by talking about how this company got started. The core technology grew out of a research project at Berkeley, correct? How did it all come about?

Ali Ghodsi: Yes. Around 2009, all the cofounders of Databricks were at U.C. Berkeley, and we were academic researchers. We had this big insight: We realized computers are not going to get any faster. We’ve hit something called Moore’s Wall: Moore’s Law no longer applies. Basically this means computers are not going to get faster every 18 months anymore.

So that meant you can’t buy supercomputers anymore to keep up with your data-analysis demands. There’s a new computer—it’s the datacenter in the cloud. We thought this was a green-field opportunity and we were very excited to pursue it. We needed to figure out, how can we now use these hundreds of thousands of machines in the cloud to process all this data and get more insights out of it, and to do predictions on it using techniques like machine learning and other artificial intelligence approaches.

Four or five years in, around 2013, we started a research project called “Spark” at UC Berkeley that later became Apache Spark to solve this. With the fast-moving evolution of the data lake, Billy Bosworth and Ali Ghodsi share their mutual thoughts on the top 5 common questions they get asked about data warehouses, data lakes and lakehouses. Coming from different backgrounds, they each provide unique and valuable insights into this market. Ali has spent more than 10 years on the forefront of research into distributed data management systems; is an adjunct professor at UC Berkeley; and is the co-founder and now CEO of Databricks. Billy has spent 30 years in the world of data as a developer, database administrator and author; has served as CEO and senior executive at software companies specializing in databases; has served on public company boards, and is currently the CEO of Dremio.

Ali Ghodsi Let’s start with one good thing before we get to the problems. They enabled enterprises to capture all their data – video/audio/logs – not just the relational data, and they did so in a cheap and open way. Today, thanks to this, the vast majority of the data, especially in the cloud, is in data lakes. Because they’re based on open formats and standards (e.g. Parquet and ORC), there is also a vast ecosystem of tools, often open sourced (e.g. Tensorflow, Pytorch), which can directly operate on these data lakes. But at some point, just collecting data for the sake of collecting it is not useful, and nobody cares about how many petabytes you’ve collected, but what have you done for the business? What business value did you provide?

It turned out it was hard to provide business value because the data lakes often became data swamps. This was primarily due to three factors. First, it was hard to guarantee that the quality of the data was good because data was just dumped into it. - Ali Ghodsi, CEO of Databricks, a cloud-based data and AI company, says AI is "eating software."
- As generative AI becomes more ubiquitous, Ghodsi says companies that prioritize data will have an advantage.
- Ghodsi says it will be so disruptive, in five to 10 years most CEOs will need some background in AI.
- This story is part of " What's Next?," a series where we ask CEOs of prominent companies across industries about how rapidly evolving trends influence their approach to leadership.

This as-told-to essay is based on a conversation with Ali Ghodsi, CEO of cloud-based data and AI company Databricks. It has been edited and condensed for clarity.

Marc Andreessen wrote the article " Why Software Is Eating the World" in 2011. I think it's obvious now that software has eaten the world.

What he meant by that is that you'll find software everywhere. Electric cars today are just fancy computers, your Nest thermostat is a piece of software, your watch is a piece of software, and your phone is a piece of software. Everything we do has become digitized, running on software. Every company is now a software company.

In every industry, software is going to become extremely important. Industries that you traditionally might not have thought were software industries — healthcare, retail, finance — all of it is software. Andreessen was very prescient in 2011. He articulated well that software companies are — soon — going to run everything.

In 2013, we at Databricks started saying that AI will eat all software. Our point is that all softw

Under the hood of the AI economy with Databricks CEO Ali Ghodsi

Under the hood of the AI economy with Databricks CEO Ali Ghodsi

Key Points

- Databricks just announced new financials, revealing more about the real productivity impact of AI agents.
- Of the databases on Databricks' platform, 80% are now being built by AI agents, not people. And these aren't all tech companies. Databricks has over 20,000 customers.
- We sat down with Databricks CEO Ali Ghodsi.

Databricks just raised$7 billion in equity and debt at a $134 billion valuation. If your eyes glazed over, I don't blame you. This is the company's umpteenth mega-round, and at some point, another private company raising another pile of money stops being news.

But here's why this one matters. As Databricks inches closer to an IPO, it's starting to act more like a public company. And the financials it's releasing tells us something more interesting than the size of the check.

Of the databases on Databricks' platform, 80% are now being built by AI agents, not people. And these aren't all tech companies. Databricks has over 20,000 customers.

It's the clearest evidence yet of something we've been debating since 2026 started: AI agents aren't just writing code, they're building real software inside the world's biggest companies. And that has major implications for the trade.

We sat down with Databricks CEO Ali Ghodsi. He sits at the intersection of all of it — the models, the data, the infrastructure and the 20,000 companies actually trying to put it all to work. He sees which AI models are winning, which ones enterprises are using and how fast agents are improving, and he has a front row seat to the question the entire software industry is grappling with right now: what happens when AI can build it for you?

- Databricks CEO says the industry has already achieved AGI — AI that can reason like a human.
- Silicon Valley invented superintelligence to raise the bar, Ali Ghodsi said.
- But the fixation with superintelligence is "misdirected," and AGI is good enough for companies, he said.

Databricks CEO says we already have artificial general intelligence. Silicon Valley just refuses to admit it.

Ali Ghodsi said at Goldman Sachs' Communicopia + Technology Conference in September that AI chatbots already meet the definition of AGI — AI that can reason like a human — that researchers used a decade ago.

"Everybody would say yes, but we kept moving the goalposts," Ghodsi said in the discussion, which was published Tuesday.

"Since we kind of achieved it, let's come up with something even bigger," he added, referring to the push toward superintelligence— AI that can reason far smarter than humans.

Ghodsi, who holds a doctorate in computer science, said the industry's goal of superintelligence is nowhere close using today's techniques. He also said the fixation with superintelligence is "misdirected," adding that building systems to outsmart the world's brightest minds isn't what companies actually need.

AGI "has everything we need to be able to automate and build the agents," he said. "We just need to do the boring work," he added.

On Monday, Databricks announced it reached a $5.4 billion revenue run rate, growing 65% year-over-year, of which more than $1.4 billion was from its AI products.

Co-founder and CEO Ali Ghodsi wanted to share these growth numbers because there’s so much talk about how AI is going to kill the SaaS business, he told TechCrunch.

“Everybody’s like, ‘Oh, it’s SaaS. What’s going to happen to all these companies? What’s AI going to do with all these companies?’ For us, it’s just increasing the usage,” he said.

To be sure, he also wants to distance Databricks from the SaaS label, given that private markets value it as an AI company. Databricks on Monday also officially closed on its massive, previously announced $5 billion raise at a $134 billion valuation, and nabbed a $2 billion loan facility as well.

But the company is straddling both worlds. Databricks is still best known as a cloud data warehouse provider. A data warehouse is where enterprises store massive amounts of data to analyze for business insights.

Ghodsi called out, in particular, one AI product that’s driving usage of its data warehouse: its LLM user interface named Genie.

Genie is an example of how a SaaS business can replace its user interface with natural language. For instance, he uses it to ask why warehouse usage and revenue spike on particular days.

Just a few years ago, such a request required writing queries in a specific technical language, or having a special report programmed. Today, any product with an LLM interface can be used by anyone, Ghodsi noted. Genie is one reason for the company’s usa

- Fast forward to 2024, Databricks has 8,000 employees and $2.5B in Annualized Revenue Run-Rate

Ali on PLG versus Sales at Databricks

> We launched an effort called zero touch, which meant we are going to touch our customers zero times. We instructed all the sales teams to stop talking to their customers and focus everything on just making the product seamless. We thought then everything would just grow by itself. Revenue was kind of increasing [initially], and then it got flat for two quarters. I think we all then realized, oh shit, that's not gonna work.”

- Ali looks for five ingredients in every leader he hires: 1) vision, 2) strategy, execution, 4) hiring, and 5) culture
- He views execution and team as the most important factors followed by strategy, vision, and culture
- A high-performing team executing quickly and effectively can compensate for mistakes in strategy
- You should rate yourself as a CEO and your direct reports on each of these five leadership attributes

- A leader must communicate a compelling vision that motivates employees, especially as the company scales. This vision helps people connect their daily tasks to a larger purpose, inspiring them to work their “asses off”

The Accidental Billionaire

INSIDE A 13TH-FLOOR BOARDROOM IN DOWNTOWN SAN FRANCISCO, THE ATMOSPHERE WAS TENSE.

It was November 2015, and Databricks, a two-year-old software company started by a group of seven Berkeley researchers, was long on buzz but short on revenue.

The directors awkwardly broached subjects that had been rehashed time and again. The startup had been trying to raise funds for five months, but venture capitalists were keeping it at arm's length, wary of its paltry sales. Seeing no other option, NEA partner Pete Sonsini, an existing investor, raised his hand to save the company with an emergency $30 million injection.

The next order of business: a new boss. Founding CEO Ion Stoica had agreed to step aside and return to his professorship at the University of California, Berkeley. The obvious move was to bring in a seasoned Silicon Valley executive, which is exactly what Databricks' chief competitor, Snowflake, did twice on its way to a software record $33 billion IPO in September 2020. Instead, at the urging of Stoica and the other cofounders, they chose Ali Ghodsi, the cofounder who was then working as vice president of engineering.

"Some of the rest of the board was naturally like, 'That doesn't make any sense: Swap out one founder-professor for another?' " recalls Ben Horowitz, the company's first VC backer and himself initially skeptical of entrusting the company to a career academic with no experience running a business. A compromise was reached: Give Ghodsi a one-year trial run.

By Horowitz's own admission, Ghodsi, 42, bald and clean-shaven, has become the best CEO in Andreessen Horowitz's portfolio, which spans hu

Ali Ghodsi, Co-Founder and CEO of Databricks | Goldman Sachs

Ali Ghodsi, Co-Founder and CEO of Databricks | Goldman Sachs

Talks at GS

Jul 16, 2021

Share

In this episode of Talks at GS, Ali Ghodsi, co-founder and CEO of Databricks, discusses how his personal journey has influenced his entrepreneurial and leadership style, his company’s mission to build the future of data and AI for all enterprises, and key insights to how Databricks drives innovation within the tech landscape.

On the state of AI: “It’s very early…I know this is cliché, but Marc Andreessen said, ‘software is eating the world.’ He meant software is in your Apple Watch, it’s in your thermostat, it’s in your Tesla car, everywhere it’s software. I really think AI will follow software. Wherever you have software, you’re going to collect data and you’re going to automate things. It’s going to be more intelligence. So, you get more intelligent software…we’re still in the ‘software is eating the world’ kind of phase. So… it’s very early.

On entrepreneurial and leadership style: “Try to simulate…how they feel, or what they want, or what they want to buy, or how they feel about your products, or how they feel about how you’re treating them as employees…If you can empathize and really simulate how people are thinking, you can much better connect to them. And I think that’s really, really crucial.”

The episode was recorded on June 17, 2021.

Biohub Launches the Virtual Biology Initiative to Galvanize a Global Effort to Create the Open Data Foundation for AI-Accelerated Biology

Author: Biohub
Type: Press Release

Biohub has launched the Virtual Biology Initiative, a five-year initiative to create the Open Data Foundation for AI-Accelerated Biology. The initiative aims to use $500 million in funding to develop predictive models of the human cell to accelerate the cure and prevention of all disease. Biohub is committing $100 million to support this effort, $400 million to generate data at scale and develop next-generation technologies for measuring, imaging, and engineering biology. The data generated by Biohub will be made open and freely available as a resource for the worldwide scientific community. Other institutions involved include the Allen Institute, Arc Institute, Broad Institute, and Wellcome Sanger Institute. NVIDIA will support the initiative to leverage accelerated computing infrastructure, domain-specific software, and technical expertise to generate large-scale datasets.

>> Yeah. So we're seeing lots and lots of momentum behind two of our products. The first one is called Agent Bricks. And that product really helps you build agents that are helping out in the workplace to automate sort of work that you have to do everyday tasks. And what's different here is a lot of the AI labs have been really focused on super intelligence, you know, math olympiads and programing contests. We're more focused on just everyday tasks that you need to do. And we're seeing huge uptick in the adoption of this. So it's actually accelerating our revenue. And the second one is a database that we built called Lake Base which supports these agents because these agents all need a database to store things in. And they use these databases differently from humans. So yeah, we're actually seeing I've actually a long time been saying that, you know, there's a bubble that AI is maybe, you know, overheated.
But actually we're finally seeing the green shoots and some of these use cases actually taking off.
>> So we just talked about the research by Torsten Slok, looking at the decline among major companies in terms of that investment into AI.
Doesn't sound like you're seeing it. We also have been talking quite a bit about cash burn. Most recently, the disclosures from OpenAI and how much they're spending. You achieved positive free cash flow. Can that continue? And I guess to go back to this bubble question, do you think that we're starting to see this formulation of winners and losers and normalization as AI becomes more prevalent?
>> Yeah, I mean, so we were very careful with this. You know, should we also invest, should we get insane number of GPUs and so on. And that's when we decided, no, we're going to run this like a public company but in private markets. So that's why 12 last months we were cash flow positive and for a long time have been saying we should be careful. Let's not get overexcited about AI. And, you know, I think there was an over promise and people overinvested and now it's kind of coming back down. But actually the thing that's so interesting is that just now the actual use cases are starting to work and they're actually starting to get deployed. So interesting to see what happens with these expectations. You know, can we catch up with these production use cases of AI that are just starting to catch up, or is it just there's too much excitement and people overspent and there's going to be a correction.
>> On that. Ali, first of all, good to see you. It's been a while. It seems before customers can get the benefit of AI Agentic AI, they have to have their data organized, tagged, managed. I'm hearing that is especially a challenge for larger and older enterprises that have lots of data silos from acquisitions, lots of unstructured data. How much of a bottleneck is that to AI adoption and monetization?
How do you clear it?
>> Yeah, that's actually a great point, John. It's actually the governance of the data that's the problem. It's not like we can't access that data. That data is there. So why are we just not using it then for AI? It's because of privacy. Because at the other side of this, corporations are super worried about their most sensitive data. They want to be really careful who gets access to it, you know, is it going to get hacked? Are we going to have a leak? So that's also hampering access. So then how do you govern that data in a secure, safe way, in a privacy preserving way? This is why we focus a lot on that. At Databricks, we built a whole solution called Unity Catalog that just does that. And it's actually the number one sort of prerequisite before you can use AI. So that's the other. That's the other side of this. That's what holding back the AI because people are nervous about their data.
>> So let me get to this lake base product as well. Help me understand this conundrum that I see potentially with large customer adoption. There's some of your highest potential customers, but they're also likely to have databases they've been working with for a long time, like like Oracle, which reports tomorrow, maybe MongoDB for some users. Can you give an example with Lake Base of how the adoption cycle with large customers plays out, how you expect to gain share?
>> Yeah, that's an excellent question. For the last 40 years, this database market hasn't changed that much. The thing that has actually changed is in the last three years, we're now seeing it in our data. We looked at we tracked who's creating these databases inside Databricks. And for the first time, over 80% of the databases are being created by AI agents.
So for the first time in the history of mankind, it's no longer humans. They used to be called database administrators, DBAs. They're not the ones creating the databases. It's a new customer. It's the agent.
So we're really focused on helping them. And we think that's the way you can actually disrupt that whole database market. What's really happening in the market, John, is that previously enterprises were buying SaaS applications, and these SaaS applications could do all kinds of things with a system of record data that they had. But more and more, what's happening now is that enterprises are saying, you know, we'll just vibe code using AI, our own software, ourselves, and that vibe coded software needs a database behind it, and it needs to be really good for the vibe coded agents that they're building.
That's where Lake Based comes in. So I think you're going to see this trend more and more, not just about our lake base, but that in the industry as a whole, enterprises are building their own software software in-house, using vibe coding, because the costs of building software has just come down dramatically now, thanks to AI.
>> Ali, before we let you go, you said you've been running the company like a public company. Any plans to IPO?
>> Well, you know, as I said, you know, if we were planning on long term being private, we would have been burning 10 billion a year or something. So we're not doing that. So the fact that we're cash flow breakeven means that, you know, we want to run it like a public company. We have public company financial audits that happen, but we're not in a hurry. Also, at the same time, we just have there's so much demand for our software. Just keeping up in the AI space is

On June 11, 2025, during the Data+AI Summit 2025 Keynote in San Francisco, Jamie Dimon, Chairman and CEO of JPMorgan Chase, and Ali Ghodsi, CEO of Databricks, engaged in a conversation about the transformative role of data and AI in the financial sector. Dimon highlighted the immense challenge of managing data, noting that JPMorgan Chase processes $10 trillion daily, stating, “The hardest part is the data. It dwarfs everything.” The discussion explored AI strategies, the need for workforce upskilling, and enterprise priorities, aligning with the summit’s theme, “Data Intelligence for All.”
Dimon stressed that managing vast data volumes, such as JPMorgan’s $10 trillion daily transactions, is a bigger hurdle than AI technology itself. This underscores the need for robust data infrastructure to support AI initiatives.
The talk covered JPMorgan’s approach to integrating AI, including modernizing data preparation with Databricks’ platform, and the need to upskill employees to use AI effectively.
Dimon discussed breaking down internal barriers to fast-track AI adoption while maintaining compliance, offering insights into strategic priorities for financial institutions.

Ben Horowitz is the co-founder of Andreessen Horowitz, Silicon Valley’s largest and most influential venture capital firm, with over $46B in committed capital across multiple funds. He took Loudcloud public with just $2 million in revenue (dubbed “the IPO from hell”), sold it for $1.6 billion, and has backed companies from Facebook to Stripe to Airbnb to OpenAI to Databricks (now worth more than $100 billion). His management philosophy—forged through near-death experiences and refined through coaching hundreds of CEOs—contradicts most conventional startup wisdom.
*In our conversation, Ben shares:*
1. Why “founder mode” is half right and half dangerously wrong
2. The story behind “Good Product Manager/Bad Product Manager” and why it went viral despite being written in anger
3. Where the biggest AI startup opportunities remain
4. Why you need to run toward fear, never away
5. The one trait that predicts that a founder will fail as CEO
6. Inside Paid in Full, Ben’s nonprofit awarding pensions to pioneering hip-hop artists

Author: Ali Hassan

Spark does not immediately execute transformations, but builds a logical execution plan (DAG), which allows Spark to optimize the entire query. DAG stands for Directed Acyclic Graph and helps Spark optimize the execution plan and provide fault tolerance. Narrow and Wide transformations are different operations that require data shuffling across the network. Data is only processed when an Action (like or ) is called. Data Skew can cause one node to perform 90% of the work while others sit idle. The default storage level (MEMORYAND_DISK) can be specified. The DataFrame is high-level, schema-based (like tables), highly optimized by Catalyst, and Dataset is strictly typed DataFrames. However, these errors can lead to significant performance overhead.

Author: Ashutosh Singhal

The Eightfold AI lawsuit, Kistler v. Eightfold, has been brought to court alleging that the company secretly profiles individuals and uses their profiles to determine their economic futures. The lawsuit argues that Eightfold’s platform uses data from LinkedIn, GitHub, Crunchbase, and other public sources to create a “match score” from 0 to 5 that predicts your “likelihood of success.” Companies like Morgan Stanley, Starbucks, BNY, and PayPal used these scores to filter candidates before a human recruiter ever glanced at an application. The case argues that if Eightfold generates match scores based on these data, it is entitled to full rights that every candidate it scored was entitled to disclosure, and dispute. The complaint alleges that the behavior of third parties compiling profiles is illegal.

- PayPal Holdings, Inc.
- Crunchbase
- Starbucks Corporation
- Equifax Inc.

Join us as we dive into the world of AI at LlamaCon 2025. This session features a keynote presentation with Chief Product Officer Chris Cox, VP of Llama Manohar Paluri, and Llama Researcher Angela Fan. Together, they will explore the importance of open-source collaboration and celebrate the achievements of the developer community. Discover the latest advances in Llama and get a glimpse of what's next in AI innovation.
Following the keynote, don't miss the fireside chat with Meta Founder and CEO Mark Zuckerberg and Databricks Co-founder and CEO Ali Ghodsi. They will discuss the critical role of open-source AI and share valuable advice for founders building AI-enabled applications.
